retrieved_docs.title: Mycosis Fungoides -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Mycosis fungoides is an incurable disorder unless the patient has very early stage disease. The mortality and morbidity increase as the tumor advances. While early-stage patients have a 95% survival over ten years, those who advanced cancer only have a 3-4 year survival. Those with the advanced extra-cutaneous disease only have a survival of fewer than 18 months. Poor prognostic factors include male gender, advanced age, and elevated LDH. [39] [33] (Level V)

retrieved_docs.title: Mycosis Fungoides -- Treatment / Management -- Advanced Stage
retrieved_docs.content: For most patients with cutaneous tumors involving a limited percent body surface area (typically less than 10%), treatment with localized radiation to the tumors plus skin-directed therapy as needed for concurrent patch/plaque disease is recommended. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Mycosis Fungoides -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the various clinical stages of mycosis fungoides. Describe the histopathology of mycosis fungoides. Outline the treatment of mycosis fungoides. Explain the importance of collaboration and communication among the interprofessional team to ensure mycosis fungoides is appropriately treated thereby improving outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Mycosis Fungoides -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: It is easy to confuse mycosis fungoides for common skin disorders such as eczema, psoriasis, parapsoriasis, photodermatitis, or drug reactions. The important differentials to keep in mind include: [36] Atopic dermatitis, contact dermatitis, drug eruptions, or erythrodermic psoriasis, all of which may cause pruritis and erythroderma resembling mycosis fungoides Psoriasis Sezary syndrome which may resemble erythrodermic mycosis fungoides Other lymphomas such as subcutaneous panniculitis-like T cell lymphoma, primary cutaneous anaplastic large cell lymphoma, cutaneous gamma/delta T cell lymphoma, or cutaneous B cell lymphoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Mycosis Fungoides -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The early identification, diagnosis and management of Mycosis fungoides is imperative in a dermatology out patient setting. This activity reviews the etiolopathogenesis, epidemiology, clinical and histological features, diagnosis and treatment of Mycosis fungoides.

retrieved_docs.title: Lichen Simplex Chronicus -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The following conditions should be excluded: psoriasis, atopic dermatitis, lichen planus, contact dermatitis, mycosis fungoides, fungal infections, and squamous cell carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Mycosis Fungoides -- Staging -- Skin
retrieved_docs.content: T1: Limited patches, papules, and/or plaques  covering less than 10% of the skin surface. May further stratify into T1a (patch only) vs. T1b (plaque +/- patch). T2: Patches, papules or plaques covering 10% or more of the skin surface. May further stratify into T2a (patch only) vs. T2b (plaque +/- patch). T3: One or more tumors  (1 cm or more in diameter) T4: Confluence of erythema covering 80% or more of body surface area

retrieved_docs.title: Mycosis Fungoides -- Treatment / Management -- Advanced Stage
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with generalized tumors, total skin electron beam therapy (TSEBT) or systemic therapies are acceptable treatment options. TSEBT can be followed by other skin-directed therapies or systemic therapies to prolong response duration. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Mycosis Fungoides -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of mycosis fungoides and Sezary syndrome is variable. The TNMB staging is the strongest prognostic factor. The following factors may be associated with a poor prognosis: [5] Presence of extracutaneous disease (stage IV), Older age (greater than 60), Presence of large cell transformation (LCT), and Increased LDH Folliculotropic mycosis fungoides Tumor distribution at diagnosis

retrieved_docs.title: Mycosis Fungoides -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Primary cutaneous lymphomas are the second most common extranodal non-Hodgkin Lymphomas. They may be of either T cell, B cell, or NK cell origin. Cutaneous T Cell lymphomas (CTCL) comprise a group of heterogeneous lymphomas which clinically differ from systemic lymphomas, even though they might show similar histology. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Mycosis Fungoides -- Treatment / Management -- Early stage:
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with highly symptomatic, generalized thickened plaques in whom a prompt response is needed, the recommendation is total skin electron beam therapy (TSEBT) or skin-directed therapies in combination with systemic therapies rather than HN2 or UVB alone. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Sezary Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mycosis fungoides tend to affect the older population. Generally, this condition predominately occurs in patients aged older than 50 and is more common in men compared to women, with a ratio of 2:1. The incidence of Sezary syndrome in the United States is about 0.8 to 0.9 cases per million persons per year. Like mycosis fungoides, this condition commonly affects the older adult population and men. The incidence is higher in White populations than Black populations. There is no genetic predisposition and thus no increased risk of developing Sezary syndrome in families of affected patients. [7]

retrieved_docs.content: EBVMCU (provisional): Appears as shallow, well-circumscribed mucosal or cutaneous ulcers with indolent disease progression. Most patients experience spontaneous regression. retrieved_docs.title: Mycosis Fungoides -- Treatment / Management -- Advanced Stage
retrieved_docs.content: For more extensive disease, combinations of skin-directed therapies and systemic therapies are used. The various systemic options available include methotrexate, bexarotene, targeted immunotherapy (such as alemtuzumab), polychemotherapy, etc. [34]

Recognizing that mycoses frequently affect the kožní systém is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate antifungal therapy, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1642
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE constitute a broad class of antifungal agents employed in the treatment of skin disorders, and within this class, antifungals for topical use represent the specific subset of medications applied directly to the skin surface to manage superficial fungal infections. Recognizing that topical antifungals are a component of the larger dermatological antifungal category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy based on the site and severity of infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2494
retrieved_docs.content: Triazole derivatives, systemic antimycotics constitute a class of antifungal agents that are administered systemically to treat invasive fungal infections. Both itraconazole and voriconazole are members of this triazole derivative class, sharing the characteristic triazole ring structure that confers broad-spectrum activity against a variety of pathogenic fungi. Their classification as systemic antimycotics underscores their use in managing serious fungal diseases where topical therapy is insufficient, highlighting the importance of these agents in comprehensive antifungal treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1642
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE represent a therapeutic class that includes both ANTIFUNGALS FOR TOPICAL USE and ANTIFUNGALS FOR SYSTEMIC USE. By definition, topical antifungal agents are applied directly to the skin, nails, or scalp to treat superficial fungal infections, whereas systemic antifungal agents are administered orally or intravenously to reach deeper or more extensive dermatologic sites. The inclusion of both topical and systemic formulations under the umbrella of ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE reflects the clinical reality that dermatologic fungal conditions can be managed with either local therapy for mild or localized disease or systemic therapy for more severe, widespread, or refractory infections. This hierarchical relationship underscores the importance of selecting the appropriate route of administration based on infection severity, pathogen susceptibility, and patient factors, ensuring comprehensive care for dermatologic fungal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2862
retrieved_docs.content: Imidazole and triazole derivatives, topical antifungals represent a pharmacologic class of antifungal agents that includes the specific drugs isoconazole, omoconazole, and tioconazole. All three compounds share the imidazole or triazole chemical scaffold and are formulated for topical use to treat superficial fungal infections. Their classification within the same group highlights their similar mechanisms of action, spectrum of activity, and therapeutic applications in dermatologic antifungal therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2178
retrieved_docs.content: The medical concept “سرطان الجلد الخبيث” (malignant skin cancer) is mapped from the broader description “ورم ثانوي خبيث في مواضع محددة أخرى” (malignant secondary tumor in other specific locations), indicating that malignant skin cancer is understood as a form of malignant secondary tumor that can arise in particular anatomical sites. This relationship clarifies that the skin manifestation of a malignant secondary tumor is classified under the specific term “سرطان الجلد الخبيث,” linking the general category of secondary malignant neoplasms to its specific cutaneous presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 658
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE is a broad category that includes both antifungals for topical use and antifungals for systemic use, each representing a distinct subclass within dermatologic antifungal therapy. Antifungals for topical use are applied directly to the skin to treat superficial fungal infections, while antifungals for systemic use are administered orally or intravenously to address deeper or more extensive dermatologic fungal conditions. Terbinafin is an example of a drug that belongs to the systemic antifungal subgroup used in dermatology, illustrating how specific agents are classified within the larger framework of antifungal treatments for skin disorders.
